descriptions.description:
Here's a description of what's visible in the screenshot:

The screen shows a web browser displaying a Miro board titled "Functions". The board has three bullet points listed: "reusability", "abstraction", and "modularity".  There is also a tab open for "p5.js Web Editor" and another for "CanvasRenderingContext2D". The canvas itself is black with no visible elements.  The URL in the address bar indicates a Miro board ID: `app.miro.com/board/uXJvLSBygz0=/`.

Here's a description of what's visible in the screenshot:

The screen shows a Miro board titled "Functions" with three bullet points listed: "reusability", "abstraction", and "modularity". A sidebar on the left contains icons for adding text, shapes, notes, apps, and other Miro board elements.  A dark-themed p5.js Web Editor is open in a tab, but the code within it is not visible due to its small size. The browser address bar indicates a URL related to Miro and an app ID, suggesting it's likely part of an interactive coding exercise.  The bottom toolbar shows p5.js controls (play, step, stop).

The screen displays a web development instructional video interface, specifically focused on the p5.js library within Mozilla's Web APIs documentation. The main area shows a code editor with the `CanvasRenderingContext2D.ellipse()` method documentation and example code. A canvas preview displays an elliptical arc with a red stroke, originating from the center of the canvas and extending clockwise. 

Here's the code shown:

```js
ellipse(x, y, radiusX, radiusY, rotation, startAngle, endAngle, counterclockwise)

// The ellipse() method creates an elliptical arc centered at (x, y) with the radii radiusX and
// radiusY. The path starts at startAngle and ends at endAngle, and travels in the direction given by
// counterclockwise (defaulting to clockwise).

Parameters
x	The x-axis (horizontal) coordinate of the ellipse's center.
y	Canvas
```

The UI includes tabs for "References", "Web APIs", and a search bar.  A sidebar lists functions like `fillRect()`, `fillText()`, and `getContextAttributes()`.  A "BugHerd" widget is visible in the lower right corner, indicating a feedback tool.

Here's a description of what's visible in the screenshot:

The screen shows a Miro board being used as part of an instructional coding video, likely related to p5.js based on the toolbar at the bottom. The main content of the board is a list titled "Functions" with three bullet points: "reusability", "abstraction", and "modularity".  There are green arrow icons next to each bullet point. 

A p5.js Web Editor is open in a browser tab, but the code within it is not visible as the editor window appears minimized. The bottom toolbar of p5.js shows standard controls like run, stop, and settings.  The browser tab title indicates the URL `miro.com/app/board/uXJVLSLBygz0=`.
